5:0.rendering: “Be careful not to perform your acts of righteousness before people in order to be seen by them. Otherwise you have no reward from your Father who is in the heavens.”
5:0.note: The Greek word for “be careful” (prosechete) echoes warnings against the evil eye of envy in the ancient world, flipping the focus from guarding against others’ greed to guarding your own heart from performing for their applause.

5:1.rendering: So when you give to the needy, do not sound a trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, so that they may be praised by others. Truly, I tell you, they have received their reward.
5:1.note: The Greek word for “trumpet” here is a metaphor for self-promotion, echoing how ancient benefactors announced gifts with fanfare.

5:2.rendering: But when you give to the needy, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing,
5:2.note: Jesus borrows a vivid proverb of total secrecy, urging generosity so instinctive it evades even your own self-congratulation.

5:3.rendering: So that your acts of mercy may be done in secret, and your Father who sees in secret will repay you.
5:3.note: The Greek word for "in secret" echoes the hidden manna of Exodus 16, where God alone sees and provides.

5:4.rendering: And when you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, for they love to stand and pray in the synagogues and at the street corners so that they may be seen by others. Truly I tell you, they have received their reward in full.
5:4.note: The word for "hypocrites" originally meant stage actors, exposing how public prayer can become religious performance.

5:5.rendering: But you, when you pray, go into your inner room, shut your door, and pray to your Father who is in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you.
5:5.note: The Greek "tamieion" evokes a hidden storeroom, turning private prayer into a quiet act of trust that God alone sees and answers.

5:6.rendering: And when you pray, do not heap up empty phrases like the Gentiles do, for they think that they will be heard because of their many words.
5:6.note: The Greek battalogeo suggests stammering or babbling, echoing the endless ritual chants of pagan prayer in the ancient world.

5:7.rendering: Do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you ask him.
5:7.note: This echoes the ancient conviction that the God of Israel sees the heart, not just the lips, long before the prayer is spoken.

4:0.rendering: When Jesus saw the crowds, he went up the mountain. After he sat down, his disciples came to him.
4:0.note: This opening echoes Moses ascending Sinai to deliver the law, setting Jesus as the new lawgiver on the mountain.

4:1.rendering: He opened his mouth and began to teach them, saying:
4:1.note: This formula, "he opened his mouth," echoes how God spoke through the prophets in the Old Testament, signaling that Jesus now speaks with divine authority.

4:2.rendering: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of the heavens.
4:2.note: This beatitude echoes Isaiah 61:1, where the anointed one brings good news to the poor in spirit, the very ones now inheriting God's reign.

4:3.rendering: Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
4:3.note: The word for "mourn" here echoes Isaiah 61:2-3, where God's anointed one comforts all who mourn and gives them a garland instead of ashes.

4:4.rendering: Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.
4:4.note: This echoes Psalm 37:11, where the meek who wait on the Lord and trust him amid evil will inherit the renewed land.

4:5.rendering: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied.
4:5.note: The Greek for "satisfied" is the same word used when Jesus feeds the crowds with loaves and fish, turning a beatitude into a promise of messianic abundance.

4:6.rendering: Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy.
4:6.note: The wordplay in Greek (eleemones...eleethesontai) echoes the Hebrew root for mercy (rahamim), linking character to covenant reciprocity in the ancient world.

4:7.rendering: Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.
4:7.note: In the ancient world, the heart was the seat of desire and decision, so purity here means an undivided will, not just clean thoughts, echoing Psalm 24's call for clean hands and a pure heart to ascend the hill of the Lord.

6:0.rendering: Do not judge, so that you will not be judged.
6:0.note: This echoes the ancient principle of measure for measure, a thread running from Leviticus to Jesus' own teaching on mercy.

6:1.rendering: For with the judgment you judge others, you will be judged, and with the measure you measure out, it will be measured back to you.
6:1.note: This echoes the ancient principle of reciprocity found in the Torah, turning it inward as a mirror for self-examination rather than a weapon against others.

6:2.rendering: Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log in your own eye?
6:2.note: The Greek wordplay between karphos (speck of dust or straw) and dokos (beam or log from a house) turns a carpenter's image into a razor-sharp jab at self-deception.

6:3.rendering: Or how can you say to your brother, 'Let me take the speck out of your eye,' when there is a log in your own eye?

6:4.rendering: You hypocrite, first take the plank out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take the speck out of your brother's eye.
6:4.note: The Greek word for "hypocrite" originally meant a stage actor, exposing how we perform righteousness while ignoring our own glaring faults.

6:5.rendering: Do not give what is holy to the dogs, and do not throw your pearls before pigs, or they will trample them under their feet, turn and tear you to pieces.
6:5.note: This vivid proverb plays on ancient Jewish images of dogs and pigs as unclean scavengers, warning that some will reject and attack the sacred gifts you offer.

6:6.rendering: Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and it will be opened to you.
6:6.note: These three imperative verbs form a rhythmic ladder of persistence in the original Greek, echoing the ancient Near Eastern idea that bold, ongoing pursuit aligns the seeker with God's generous character.

6:7.rendering: For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened.
6:7.note: The three parallel verbs, ask seek knock, echo the urgency of Proverbs 2:1-5 where wisdom is found by those who search for her like hidden treasure.

4:8.rendering: Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.
4:8.note: The Greek word for peacemakers echoes the Hebrew shalom-bringers, those who actively repair broken relationships, and Jesus places them in the family line of the God who reconciles all things.

4:9.rendering: Blessed are those who are persecuted on account of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of the heavens.
4:9.note: This final beatitude echoes the first (v. 3) with the exact same promise of the kingdom, forming a literary bracket around the whole set.

4:10.rendering: Blessed are you when they insult you and persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you on account of me.
4:10.note: The word for "falsely" echoes the ninth commandment against false witness, turning unjust accusation into the very place where God's blessing rests.

4:11.rendering: Rejoice and be glad, for your reward is great in the heavens, for in this way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.
4:11.note: The call to rejoice echoes the same rare Greek word used for the joy at Jesus' birth in Luke 2:10, linking the persecuted to the ancient line of prophets.

4:12.rendering: You are the salt of the earth. But if the salt loses its savor, how can it be made salty again? It is no longer good for anything except to be thrown out and trampled under people's feet.
4:12.note: In the ancient world, salt preserved, seasoned, and sealed covenants, your calling is to bring flavor and prevent decay in the world around you.

4:13.rendering: You are the light of the world. A city built on a hill cannot be hidden.
4:13.note: Jesus echoes Isaiah 42:6 and 49:6, where Israel was meant to be a light to the nations, now fulfilled in his gathered community.

4:14.rendering: No one lights a lamp and puts it under a basket. Instead they put it on a lampstand, and it gives light to everyone in the house.
4:14.note: The Greek for "basket" (modios) is a grain-measure vessel, turning Jesus' image into a vivid picture of hidden light that makes no sense in a peasant home.

4:15.rendering: In the same way, let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven.
4:15.note: The Greek verb for "shine" echoes Genesis 1's creation of light, inviting Jesus' followers to participate in God's original act of making the world see his goodness.

5:8.rendering: So pray like this: Our Father in the heavens, let your name be made holy.
5:8.note: The opening line echoes the ancient Jewish prayer tradition, where invoking God's fatherhood and the sanctifying of his name pulls together Israel's covenant hope with the longing that his character be honored on earth as in heaven.

5:9.rendering: May your kingdom come, may your will be done, as in heaven, so also on earth.
5:9.note: This pairs two parallel pleas, echoing the ancient Jewish prayer that God's rule would break into the present age just as it already holds sway in the heavenly court.

5:10.rendering: Give us today our daily bread.
5:10.note: The Greek word for "daily" (epiousios) is rare and mysterious, likely meaning "for the coming day," echoing Israel's wilderness manna that could not be hoarded.

5:11.rendering: And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors.
5:11.note: The Greek word for debts (opheilemata) carries both financial and moral obligation, echoing the ancient practice of canceling loans every seven years in Israel.

5:12.rendering: And do not bring us into the test, but rescue us from the evil one.
5:12.note: The word for "test" here echoes the wilderness trial of Israel and Jesus himself, inviting us to pray for rescue rather than presumption.

5:13.rendering: For if you forgive people their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.
5:13.note: This verse echoes the fifth petition of the Lord's Prayer, linking our horizontal forgiveness to God's vertical mercy in a single, reciprocal rhythm.

5:14.rendering: But if you do not forgive others their offenses, neither will your Father forgive your offenses.
5:14.note: This mirrors the ancient reciprocity principle, echoing the Lord's Prayer just verses before.

5:15.rendering: When you fast, do not look gloomy like the hypocrites do. They disfigure their faces so that people will notice they are fasting. Truly I tell you, they have received their reward in full.
5:15.note: The Greek word for "disfigure" literally means "to make invisible" or "to ruin the appearance," creating a sharp ironic contrast with their desire to be seen by others.

6:8.rendering: Or what person is there among you who, if his son asks him for bread, will give him a stone instead?
6:8.note: This rhetorical question echoes the ancient cultural expectation of a good father providing basic sustenance, setting up the contrast with how much more God gives good gifts.

6:9.rendering: Or if he asks for a fish, will he give him a snake instead?
6:9.note: This pairs with the bread-and-stone question in v.9, using everyday Galilean foods and dangers to expose how a good father instinctively gives what nourishes, not what harms.

6:10.rendering: So if you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in the heavens give good things to those who ask him?
6:10.note: The "how much more" argument echoes a common rabbinic style of reasoning from the lesser to the greater, here turning parental instinct into a window on the Father's generosity.

6:11.rendering: So whatever you want people to do for you, do that for them too. For this is the Law and the Prophets.
6:11.note: This golden rule echoes the Torah's command to love your neighbor as yourself, wrapping up the entire Hebrew Scriptures in one active principle.

6:12.rendering: Enter through the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the road is broad that leads to destruction, and many are those who travel it.
6:12.note: The Greek word for "narrow" (stenos) evokes a tight mountain pass, contrasting the easy Roman highways that promised quick travel but led to ruin.

6:13.rendering: For the gate is narrow and the road is hard that leads to life, and there are few who find it.
6:13.note: The Greek word for "hard" (thlibo) means "pressed together" or afflicted, evoking the image of a constricted mountain pass that squeezes travelers toward life.

6:14.rendering: Watch out for the false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inside are ravenous wolves.
6:14.note: The image of wolves in sheepskins echoes ancient warnings and Jesus' own earlier words about being sent as sheep among wolves, flipping the danger inside the community.

6:15.rendering: You will recognize them by their fruits. Do they gather grapes from thornbushes, or figs from thistles?
6:15.note: Jesus echoes the Hebrew prophets' image of Israel as a vineyard that should produce good fruit, but often yields only wild or thorny growth instead.

4:16.rendering: Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets. I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them.
4:16.note: The word "fulfill" carries the weight of the Hebrew Scriptures' own hope: Jesus completes what the Torah and Prophets were always pointing toward.

4:17.rendering: For truly I tell you, until heaven and earth pass away, not one letter or one stroke of a letter will pass from the Law until everything comes to pass.
4:17.note: Jesus anchors his teaching in the enduring authority of Torah, using the smallest Hebrew letter (yod) and its decorative stroke to say not the tiniest detail will fail until the whole story reaches its goal.

4:18.rendering: Therefore whoever relaxes one of the least of these commands and teaches others to do the same will be called least in the kingdom of heaven. But whoever does them and teaches them will be called great in the kingdom of heaven.
4:18.note: This verse echoes the ancient scribal practice of ranking commandments by importance, yet Jesus insists even the "least" must be kept and passed on with integrity.

4:19.rendering: For I tell you, unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.
4:19.note: Jesus echoes Deuteronomy's call for a heart-circumcised righteousness that goes far deeper than the external fence-building of the scribes and Pharisees.

4:20.rendering: You have heard that it was said to the ancients, 'You shall not murder,' and 'Whoever murders will be liable to judgment.'
4:20.note: Jesus reaches back to the sixth commandment and its ancient judicial expansions, setting the stage for his deeper diagnosis of anger as the root that already incurs the same liability.

4:21.rendering: But I tell you that anyone who is angry with his brother will be liable to the court. And whoever says to his brother, 'Empty-head,' will be liable to the Sanhedrin. And whoever says, 'Fool,' will be liable to the fire of Gehenna.
4:21.note: Jesus echoes the ancient courtroom language of liability to judgment, escalating from local court to supreme council to the fiery valley of Hinnom, to show uncontrolled anger already puts us on trial.

4:22.rendering: So if you are offering your gift at the altar, and there remember that your brother has something against you,
4:22.note: This echoes the ancient temple practice of pausing sacrifice for relational repair, a wisdom thread that runs from Cain and Abel to the prophets.

4:23.rendering: leave your gift there in front of the altar, and first go and be reconciled to your brother, and then come and offer your gift.
4:23.note: Jesus echoes the prophets' insistence that right worship requires right relationships first, a thread running from Leviticus through Isaiah to the Sermon on the Mount.

6:16.rendering: So every healthy tree bears good fruit, but the rotten tree bears bad fruit.
6:16.note: The Greek word for "rotten" (sapros) literally means decayed or corrupt, the same term used for spoiled fish in Matthew 13:48.

6:17.rendering: A healthy tree cannot bear bad fruit, nor can a rotten tree bear good fruit.
6:17.note: The Greek word for "rotten" (sapros) echoes the decaying fruit in Jesus' earlier image of trees and their produce, exposing character by what it consistently yields.

6:18.rendering: Every tree that does not produce good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.
6:18.note: This echoes John the Baptist's warning in Matthew 3:10, linking fruitfulness to the coming judgment that Jesus now embodies.

6:19.rendering: So then, you will recognize them by their fruits.
6:19.note: The Greek word for "fruits" (karpois) echoes the garden of Eden and the promised land, where trees are known by what they produce, not their leaves.

6:20.rendering: Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of the heavens, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in the heavens.
6:20.note: Jesus echoes Deuteronomy 30: Deuteronomy's choice between life and death hinges on doing God's words, not just claiming them.

6:21.rendering: On that day many will say to me, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out demons in your name, and do many mighty works in your name?'
6:21.note: The triple repetition of "in your name" echoes the ancient idea that invoking someone's authority should guarantee results, yet here it highlights the terrifying gap between impressive activity and knowing Jesus himself.

6:22.rendering: And then I will declare to them, 'I never knew you. Depart from me, you who work lawlessness.'
6:22.note: The phrase "I never knew you" echoes the intimate covenant language of the Hebrew Bible, where "knowing" means relational belonging, not just information.

6:23.rendering: Everyone then who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock.
6:23.note: The wordplay in Greek links "these words of mine" directly back to the Sermon on the Mount, echoing Moses on Sinai.

4:24.rendering: Settle quickly with your accuser while you are still with him on the way to court, or your accuser may hand you over to the judge, the judge to the officer, and you will be thrown into prison.
4:24.note: This echoes the urgent wisdom of Proverbs, where settling disputes swiftly prevents escalating ruin.

4:25.rendering: Truly I tell you, you will never get out of there until you have paid back the last penny.
4:25.note: Jesus borrows a debtor's prison image from his day to picture inescapable consequences until every last quadrans, the smallest Roman coin, is settled.

4:26.rendering: You have heard that it was said, 'You shall not commit adultery.'
4:26.note: This launches the second of six case studies where Jesus probes the Torah's heart, echoing the Decalogue while exposing how external compliance can mask internal betrayal.

4:27.rendering: But I tell you that everyone who looks at a woman to covet her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
4:27.note: The Greek word for "looks...to covet" echoes the tenth commandment's prohibition on coveting, showing Jesus intensifying the Torah's inner demand.

4:28.rendering: If your right eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. For it is better for you that one part of your body perish than that your whole body be thrown into Gehenna.
4:28.note: The shocking hyperbole echoes ancient wisdom on radical surgery against sin, using the right eye as the most valued and pointing to Gehenna, the burning trash heap outside Jerusalem, as a vivid image of destruction.

4:29.rendering: And if your right hand causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away from you. For it is better for you that one of your parts should perish than for your whole body to go into Gehenna.
4:29.note: The shocking hyperbole echoes the right-eye command just before it, using the same "cut it off and throw it away" phrase to hammer home that radical surgery on sin is wiser than losing the whole self to the garbage-dump fire of Gehenna.

4:30.rendering: It was also said, 'Whoever sends his wife away must give her a certificate of divorce.'
4:30.note: This quotes Deuteronomy 24:1, where the certificate was meant to protect the woman from arbitrary dismissal in an ancient patriarchal culture.

4:31.rendering: But I tell you that everyone who divorces his wife, except on account of sexual immorality, makes her commit adultery, and whoever marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
4:31.note: In its first-century Jewish setting this protects the vulnerable wife from a casual certificate of divorce that could leave her destitute and labeled as an adulteress.

5:16.rendering: But when you fast, put oil on your head and wash your face,
5:16.note: This echoes the ancient practice of anointing for joy, quietly subverting public displays of mourning to keep the fast between you and God alone.

5:17.rendering: so that your fasting will not be obvious to people but to your Father who is in secret, and your Father who sees in secret will repay you.
5:17.note: The triple echo of "in secret" quietly alludes to the hidden presence of God in the Most Holy Place, turning private fasting into an act of temple-like intimacy.

5:18.rendering: Do not store up for yourselves treasures on the earth, where moth and rust destroy and where thieves break in and steal.
5:18.note: The Greek word for "treasures" (the-sau-rous) echoes the very name of the Gospel's author, Matthew, whose own tax-collecting wealth was vulnerable to exactly these forces.

5:19.rendering: But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust destroys them and where thieves do not break in and steal.
5:19.note: The word for "treasures" echoes the same term used for Joseph's storehouses in the Septuagint, turning personal wealth into a communal, imperishable provision.

5:20.rendering: For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
5:20.note: The Greek word order puts "treasure" first, then "heart," showing that our actions reveal and shape what we truly love.

5:21.rendering: The eye is the lamp of the body. So if your eye is healthy, your whole body will be full of light.
5:21.note: In Jesus' world the eye was seen as the window through which light entered the body, so a "healthy" or single eye meant a generous, undivided gaze.

5:22.rendering: But if your eye is unhealthy, your whole body will be full of darkness. So if the light in you is darkness, how great is that darkness!
5:22.note: The word for "unhealthy" here is poneros, the same term for "evil," painting a diseased eye that twists light itself into moral shadow.

5:23.rendering: No one is able to serve two masters. For either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.
5:23.note: The Aramaic word for “money” here is mammon, an ancient personification of wealth as a rival master that demands total allegiance.

6:24.rendering: The rain fell, the rivers rose, the winds blew and beat against that house, yet it did not fall, because its foundation was built on the rock.
6:24.note: The fourfold pounding, rain, rivers, winds, beating, echoes the chaotic forces of Genesis 1 and the flood, yet this house stands firm like the creation anchored by God's word.

6:25.rendering: But everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand.
6:25.note: The Greek for "foolish" echoes the wise-and-foolish contrast of Proverbs, where the fool hears but never builds his life on what he hears.

6:26.rendering: The rain fell, the floods came, the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell with a great crash.
6:26.note: The Greek word for "fell" (pipto) echoes the house's dramatic collapse like a tree in a storm, contrasting the wise builder's stability in the preceding verse.

6:27.rendering: When Jesus finished saying these words, the crowds were astounded at his teaching.
6:27.note: Matthew's phrase "finished these words" echoes the exact formula that closes each of the five books of Moses in the Greek Old Testament.

6:28.rendering: for he was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes.
6:28.note: The Greek word for authority (exousia) echoes how God himself spoke in the Torah, setting Jesus apart from scribes who merely relayed earlier rabbis.

5:24.rendering: Therefore I tell you, do not be anxious about your life, what you will eat or drink, or about your body, what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothing?
5:24.note: Jesus echoes the Creator's care in Genesis 1, where he provides food for every creature before forming humanity, inviting us to trust the same generous order.

5:25.rendering: Look at the birds of the air: they do not sow or reap or gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not worth far more than they?
5:25.note: The word for "birds" echoes Genesis 1's creation account, where God provides for every living thing before humans even appear.

5:26.rendering: And who among you, by being anxious, can add one cubit to his height?
5:26.note: The Greek word for "cubit" (pēchyn) doubles as a measure of both time and stature, letting Jesus' question echo the futility of worry in both lifespan and stature.

5:27.rendering: And why do you worry about clothing? Consider the wildflowers of the field, how they grow: they do not toil or spin thread.
5:27.note: The Greek verb katanoeo means "observe carefully" or "learn from," inviting slow, meditative attention to the lilies as wisdom teachers rather than mere illustrations.

5:28.rendering: Yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not clothed like one of these.
5:28.note: The Greek word for "clothed" (periebaleto) echoes how God "clothed" Adam and Eve in Genesis 3:21, quietly reminding us the Creator still provides better than any human king.

5:29.rendering: And if God so clothes the grass of the field, which is alive today and thrown into the oven tomorrow, will he not much more clothe you, you of little faith?
5:29.note: The phrase "of little faith" translates Jesus' single Greek word oligopistoi, a gentle rebuke that echoes his earlier wilderness testing and points forward to the disciples' stormy sea failures.

5:30.rendering: So do not worry, saying, 'What will we eat?' or 'What will we drink?' or 'What will we wear?'
5:30.note: Jesus echoes the wilderness generation's anxious grumbling about food and water, inviting his hearers to trust the God who provided manna instead.

5:31.rendering: For the nations chase after all these things, and your heavenly Father knows that you need them all.
5:31.note: The Greek word for "nations" (ethne) echoes God's ancient promise to bless all peoples through Abraham, yet here Jesus contrasts their anxious pursuit with the Father's knowing care.

4:32.rendering: Again you have heard that it was said to those of old, 'You shall not break your oath, but you shall fulfill your vows to the Lord.'
4:32.note: This echoes the ancient practice of vows in Numbers 30 and Deuteronomy 23, where God's name stakes your integrity in a world of casual promises.

4:33.rendering: But I tell you, do not swear at all: neither by heaven, for it is God's throne,
4:33.note: Jesus echoes Isaiah 66:1, where heaven is God's throne and earth his footstool, reframing oaths as an everyday claim on God's authority.

4:34.rendering: or by the earth, for it is his footstool, or by Jerusalem, for it is the city of the great king.
4:34.note: Jesus echoes Isaiah 66:1, where heaven is God's throne and earth his footstool, reframing casual oaths as claims on God's sovereign domain.

4:35.rendering: Nor shall you swear by your head, for you cannot make one hair white or black.
4:35.note: This echoes the ancient practice of invoking something precious as a guarantee, yet Jesus exposes our illusion of control even over our own bodies.

4:36.rendering: Let your yes be yes, and your no be no. Anything more than this comes from the evil one.
4:36.note: Jesus echoes the ancient call for straightforward speech, contrasting it with the deceptive oaths common in his day.

4:37.rendering: You have heard that it was said, 'An eye for an eye and a tooth for a tooth.'
4:37.note: This quotes the Old Testament principle of proportional justice from Exodus 21:24, meant to limit revenge rather than encourage it.

4:38.rendering: But I tell you, do not resist the one who is evil. Instead, if someone slaps you on the right cheek, turn the other to him as well.
4:38.note: The Greek verb for "resist" is the same one used in the Greek Old Testament for armed military revolt, flipping expectations of violent pushback.

4:39.rendering: And if someone wants to sue you and take your tunic, let him have your cloak as well.
4:39.note: The tunic was your basic undergarment, the cloak your outer one used as a blanket at night; Jesus is picturing total generosity that surrenders even what the law protected.

5:32.rendering: But seek first the kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.
5:32.note: The Greek word order puts "kingdom" and "righteousness" upfront for emphasis, echoing the Hebrew prophets' call to pursue God's rule above all.

5:33.rendering: So do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own.
5:33.note: The word for "worry" here is the same one Jesus used for the thorns choking the seed in the parable, painting anxiety as a living thing that can strangle today's fruitfulness.

4:40.rendering: And if anyone forces you to go one mile, go with him two miles.
4:40.note: Roman soldiers could legally compel civilians to carry their gear for one mile, a hated practice; Jesus urges exceeding the demand to subvert resentment with generosity.

4:41.rendering: Give to the one who asks you, and do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow from you.
4:41.note: This command echoes Deuteronomy 15:7-8 on openhanded generosity to the poor, subverting Roman reciprocity culture by removing any expectation of return.

4:42.rendering: You have heard that it was said, "You shall love your neighbor and hate your enemy."
4:42.note: This pairs the clear Leviticus 19:18 command with a common ancient assumption never actually stated in the Torah, setting up Jesus' radical expansion in the next verse.

4:43.rendering: But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.
4:43.note: Jesus turns the ancient eye-for-an-eye principle on its head, echoing Proverbs 25:21-22 and foreshadowing his own prayer from the cross.

4:44.rendering: so that you may become sons of your Father in the heavens, for he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.
4:44.note: Jesus echoes the ancient Near Eastern idea of a king as image-bearer of the sun god, but flips it: true sons reflect the Creator who gives good gifts indiscriminately to all.

4:45.rendering: For if you love only those who love you back, what reward do you have? Don't even the tax collectors do the same thing?
4:45.note: The Greek word for "reward" here echoes the wages paid to day laborers, contrasting mercenary calculation with the Father's unexpected generosity.

4:46.rendering: And if you only greet your brothers and sisters, what more are you doing than others? Do not even the Gentiles do the same?
4:46.note: The word for "greet" (aspazomai) means more than a casual hello, it is the warm embrace of kinship; Jesus asks why his followers would settle for the same limited circle the nations already practice.

4:47.rendering: Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.
4:47.note: The Greek teleios means complete, mature, or whole, echoing God's call to Israel in Leviticus 19:2 to be holy as he is holy.

7:0.rendering: When Jesus came down from the mountain, great crowds followed him.
7:0.note: This echoes Moses descending Sinai with the law, but now the authoritative teacher of a new mountain (Matt 5-7) draws crowds by his own words and presence.

7:1.rendering: A man with a skin disease came up, knelt before him, and said, "Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean."
7:1.note: In the ancient world this skin disease made him not just sick but ritually unclean and socially cut off, yet he dares to approach and kneel in confident trust.

7:2.rendering: Jesus stretched out his hand and touched him, saying, "I am willing. Be clean." And immediately his leprosy was cleansed.
7:2.note: In a world where touching a leper made you unclean, Jesus reverses the flow by touching and making clean, an echo of God's willingness to draw near what the law kept distant.

7:3.rendering: Jesus said to him, "See that you tell no one, but go, show yourself to the priest, and offer the gift that Moses commanded, as a testimony to them."
7:3.note: This command echoes Leviticus 14's ritual for cleansing skin disease, sending the man back into the very system that had excluded him.

7:4.rendering: When he entered Capernaum, a centurion came to him, pleading with him.
7:4.note: A Roman military officer, enemy of Israel, approaches Jesus as a humble supplicant in this pivotal miracle story.

7:5.rendering: Lord, my servant is lying paralyzed at home, in terrible torment.
7:5.note: The centurion's plea echoes the urgent language of lament psalms, where the sufferer is "tormented" by unseen forces beyond human control.

7:6.rendering: Jesus said to him, "I will come and heal him."
7:6.note: The Greek verb for "heal" here is the same word used in the Septuagint for God's promise to heal his people in Isaiah 53:5, quietly signaling the servant who bears our sicknesses.

7:7.rendering: The centurion answered, "Lord, I am not worthy for you to come under my roof. But only say the word, and my servant will be healed."
7:7.note: The centurion's phrase "only say the word" echoes God's creative speech in Genesis 1, revealing faith that Jesus wields the same authority.

7:8.rendering: For I too am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I say to this one, 'Go,' and he goes, and to another, 'Come,' and he comes, and to my slave, 'Do this,' and he does it.
7:8.note: The centurion's insight into authority echoes the Roman military chain, revealing how Jesus wields divine command over sickness and spirits.

7:9.rendering: Jesus was amazed when he heard this, and he said to those following him, "Truly I tell you, I have not found such great faith in anyone in Israel."
7:9.note: Matthew's Greek word for "amazed" is the same one used for the crowd's reaction to Jesus' own miracles, quietly hinting that this Gentile's trust has reversed the expected roles.

7:10.rendering: I tell you, many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven.
7:10.note: Jesus echoes Isaiah 2:2-4 and 49:12, where nations stream to the mountain of the Lord, flipping expectations about who gets a seat at the feast.

7:11.rendering: But the children of the kingdom will be thrown into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.
7:11.note: This warning echoes the prophets' imagery of exile and judgment, flipping the expectation that ethnic insiders automatically inherit the feast.

7:12.rendering: And Jesus said to the centurion, "Go. Let it be done for you just as you trusted." And the servant was healed at that very hour.
7:12.note: The word for "trusted" here is the same one used for Abraham's faith that God would give him a son against all odds, an echo of Genesis 15:6.

7:13.rendering: When Jesus came into Peter's house, he saw Peter's mother-in-law lying in bed with a fever.
7:13.note: This intimate household scene echoes the ancient expectation that a healer would enter the home, setting the stage for Jesus to restore family life through touch.

7:14.rendering: He touched her hand, and the fever left her. Then she got up and began to serve him.
7:14.note: The word for "serve" here is diakoneo, the same root that gives us deacon and the language of table-service that Jesus will later use for his own mission.

7:15.rendering: When evening came, they brought to him many who were demon-possessed, and he drove out the spirits with a word, and healed all who were sick.
7:15.note: The phrase "with a word" echoes God's creative speech in Genesis 1, showing Jesus wielding the same authoritative command over chaos and disease.

7:16.rendering: This was to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah: "He himself took our weaknesses and carried our diseases."
7:16.note: Matthew echoes Isaiah 53:4, but swaps the Hebrew "sorrows" for "diseases," showing Jesus bearing both physical sickness and the deeper brokenness of his people.

7:17.rendering: Now when Jesus saw a crowd around him, he gave orders to go across to the other side of the lake.
7:17.note: The Greek word for "gave orders" echoes the authoritative commands Jesus just gave to wind and waves in the previous scene, hinting that following him will mean crossing into chaotic waters.

7:18.rendering: Then a scribe came up and said to him, "Teacher, I will follow you wherever you go."
7:18.note: This teacher of the law addresses Jesus with the same title rabbis used for themselves, hinting at the surprising authority already radiating from the carpenter from Nazareth.

7:19.rendering: Foxes have dens and birds of the sky have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.
7:19.note: This stark image of homelessness echoes the rootlessness of the tabernacle's wanderings and the servant songs, hinting at the Messiah's total dependence on the Father.

7:20.rendering: Another of the disciples said to him, "Lord, let me first go and bury my father."
7:20.note: In a culture where honoring parents meant ensuring a proper burial, this disciple's request tests whether family duty or the urgent call of the kingdom comes first.

7:21.rendering: But Jesus said to him, "Follow me, and let the dead bury their own dead."
7:21.note: This stark command plays on the double meaning of "dead" in Greek, contrasting spiritual lifelessness with urgent discipleship in a culture where burying parents was a sacred duty.

7:22.rendering: And when he got into the boat, his disciples followed him.
7:22.note: This simple boarding scene echoes the call narratives where disciples leave everything to follow, launching a story of storm and rescue that will reveal who Jesus is.

7:23.rendering: Suddenly a violent storm arose on the sea, so that the boat was being swamped by the waves. But he was sleeping.
7:23.note: The Greek word for "storm" here is seismos, the same term used for earthquakes, evoking cosmic upheaval just as Jesus sleeps like the Lord in Psalm 121 who "will neither slumber nor sleep."

7:24.rendering: The disciples came and woke him, saying, "Lord, save us! We are perishing."
7:24.note: The Greek verb for "save" (sozo) carries the double edge of physical rescue and ultimate deliverance, echoing Israel's cries in the Psalms.

7:25.rendering: He said to them, "Why are you cowards, you of little trust?" Then he got up, rebuked the winds and the sea, and it became a great calm.
7:25.note: The single Greek word oligopistoi, "little-faith ones," is a term Jesus coins only for his disciples, linking fear of the storm to a failure to trust the Creator who once calmed the waters at the beginning.

7:26.rendering: The men were amazed and said, "What kind of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him?"
7:26.note: The astonished question echoes the ancient fear of chaotic seas and sets up the deeper riddle of Jesus identity that the Gospel will keep unfolding.

7:27.rendering: When he came to the other side, to the region of the Gadarenes, two men possessed by demons met him, coming out of the tombs. They were so violent that no one could pass by that way.
7:27.note: The Greek emphasizes their extreme ferocity, using a word that means they were so savage no traveler could get past them alive.

7:28.rendering: And look, they cried out, "What do you want with us, Son of God? Have you come here to torment us before the appointed time?"
7:28.note: The demons' terrified question echoes the ancient dread of a fixed judgment day, revealing they already know exactly who Jesus is and when their defeat is scheduled.

7:29.rendering: Now a large herd of pigs was feeding some distance away from them.
7:29.note: The pigs signal we're in Gentile territory, where this unclean herd fits the local economy but clashes with Jewish law.

7:30.rendering: The demons begged him, "If you cast us out, send us into the herd of pigs."
7:30.note: This plea echoes the ancient fear that unclean spirits craved embodiment, revealing how even evil powers must submit to Jesus' single word of permission.

7:31.rendering: He said to them, "Go." So they came out and went into the pigs, and the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned in the waters.
7:31.note: The demons' obedient exit into the pigs echoes the exodus plagues, where unclean forces are hurled into chaotic waters.

7:32.rendering: The herdsmen fled, went into the city, and reported everything, including what had happened to the demon-possessed men.
7:32.note: These unlikely witnesses, outsiders to the town, become the first evangelists of Jesus' power over darkness.

7:33.rendering: Then the whole city came out to meet Jesus. When they saw him, they begged him to leave their region.
7:33.note: The Greek verb for "begged" is the same one used by demons when they pleaded with Jesus not to torment them in verse 31, subtly equating the fearful townspeople with the unclean spirits they just witnessed expelled.
